main_summary.summary: Evans Derrell Hopkins, born 1954 in Danville, Virginia, recounts a trajectory from segregation-era childhood through Black Panther Party service to post-movement writing. He grew up in the Bruce Street neighborhood in a house his grandfather assembled brick by brick after being barred from buying on white-only West Main Street. His father Daniel Hopkins was Dan River Mills's first Black supervisor and ran a Danville landscape business. Hopkins was nine in 1963 when mass civil rights demonstrations against segregated buses, stores, and the Danville library brought wounded protesters into High Street Baptist Church and produced trials before Judge Archibald M. Aiken, who placed a pistol on the bench. He entered desegregated George Washington High School, led a 1970 student walkout demanding African American studies, and joined the BPP first as a community worker in Winston-Salem and then in 1972 as a newspaper writer in Oakland under editor David Graham Du Bois. He covered Bobby Seale's 1973 Oakland mayoral campaign and Elaine Brown's City Council run, met artists including Abbey Lincoln and photographer Stephen Shames, and worked alongside Emory Douglas. He left the party in 1974 amid Huey Newton's deterioration and internal disciplinary violence, was later imprisoned for armed robbery, and wrote his memoir Life After Life: A Story of Rage and Redemption.
key_themes: Black Panther Party national organization (Oakland editorial cadre)
Public school desegregation and student walkouts
COINTELPRO surveillance and disruption of Black radical organizing
Mass incarceration as continuation of civil rights struggle
main_summary.historical_significance: Hopkins provides a first-person inside account of the BPP's Oakland editorial operation during the 1972-74 transition from armed-struggle posture to electoral politics and community survival programs. His description of David Graham Du Bois as newspaper editor-in-chief, of the Bobby Seale mayoral campaign mechanics, and of the disciplinary culture in the party's final coherent years documents organizational history that has been undertold relative to the BPP's earlier Oakland founding period. The interview also preserves Danville-specific civil rights history: the 1963 demonstrations, the High Street Baptist Church first-aid role, Judge Archibald M. Aiken's courtroom pistol, and the post-integration removal of furniture from the Danville library as a final segregation tactic. Hopkins's reflections on COINTELPRO's documented surveillance of him personally, on the post-Panther turn toward armed robbery and incarceration, and on his memoir Life After Life: A Story of Rage and Redemption position him as a primary source for the long arc connecting Black radical organizing, federal counterintelligence, and the carceral system.

chapters.title: The One-Floor Colored Hospital
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Richmond, Virginia, Evans Derrell Hopkins describes being born in 1954 at Winslow Hospital, a one-floor facility reserved for Danville's colored citizenry, his mother Margaret Swanson Hopkins and his father, who worked for Dan River Mills as its first black supervisor of the mail room and janitorial crew before starting one of the town's few black businesses, a landscaping operation run with a tractor from the family farm.

chapters.title: The Old Jail by the A&P
chapters.summary: Hopkins credits his older sister with leading him toward the Black Panther Party and the church with drawing him toward the movement, then reads from his book Life After Life, recalling himself as a small child at the segregated A&P beside the old city jail, where in 1963 police locked away hundreds of demonstrators while firemen turned hoses on the crowds and dogs were set on protesters.

chapters.title: Lighter Than My Father
chapters.summary: Hopkins reflects on the inevitable confusion of a black child in 1963, taught that everything white was superior, and how colorism reached into his own family, his mother lighter than his darker father, himself darker than his sister, until he resolved to develop his mind and prove he was as good as his sister and, later, as good as the white students.

chapters.title: The House Moved Brick by Brick
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes his maternal grandfather, perhaps the most prominent African American in Danville, who built the houses of the Bruce Street neighborhood with his sons around the turn of the century and, denied a brick house except on the white West Main Street, bought one there and reassembled it brick by brick in Bruce Street, giving the family a big house and a sense of privilege bounded by streets they could not safely walk.

chapters.title: A Pistol Beneath the Robe
chapters.summary: Calling Danville's civil rights history rugged, Hopkins reads further from his book about the 1963 mass demonstrations against segregated buses, stores, and the city library, the bloody protesters who staggered into High Street church for first aid, Judge Archibald Aiken who placed a pistol on the bench before each session, and the library that integrated only after the courts forced it.

chapters.title: Either Integrate or Not Go at All
chapters.summary: Picking up after a short break to cover 1963 to 1969, Hopkins explains that with the nearest black county high school thirty-five miles away and no buses to reach it, his family decided the children would integrate the recently desegregated town high school, and recounts hating it there, harassed, cursed, jostled, and once attacked from behind and given a black eye, a racial bullying he later judged among the most important forces of his young life, feeding a need to resist that led him toward the movement.

chapters.title: The First Panther Paper
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes a 1970 family trip to Washington where a cousin was selling Black Panther Party newspapers, his earlier involvement in cultural nationalism with dashikis and afros, and how the party offered not only racial but class solidarity, set against a backdrop of fear of Klan terror, closed drapes, and white men who might shoot through a window out of spite.

chapters.title: We Wanted Our Own Identity
chapters.summary: Hopkins recounts planning a walkout at the integrated high school after John M. Langston High School, named for a Reconstruction-era congressman, was downgraded to a middle school, the students wanting to shed the name of George Washington, whom they saw as a slaveowner, and to win black studies classes, organized through a community-worker group that sold Black Panther newspapers from Winston-Salem.

chapters.title: A Career Begun on a Comical Footing
chapters.summary: Hopkins tells how the planned walkout was leaked early, how about 150 students including some whites walked out, how he negotiated with the principal and was kicked out of school for three days with the other organizers, and how the police did not attack them, in an atmosphere charged by the Kent State killings spilling from college campuses into the high schools.

chapters.title: The Budding Writer Reads
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes feeling he had an inordinate gift for leadership and loving the philosophy of the party, dialectical materialism as a textbook, and the Panthers' sense of a kernel of truth about the world, while reading the autobiography of Malcolm X, H. Rap Brown, Eldridge Cleaver, and the prison letters of George Jackson, and recognizing the budding writer in himself.

chapters.title: Shotgun Apartments
chapters.topic: The Authentic Culture of the Cadre
chapters.summary: Hopkins recalls being a shy young man drawn to the Panthers' fervor and culture, learning to blow on the masses, and finding the Winston-Salem members not the bereted figures of the photographs but impoverished and dedicated people sleeping on mattresses in shotgun apartments, so named because a shotgun fired through the front door would pass straight out the back.

chapters.title: Survival Till Revolution
chapters.summary: Hopkins tells of leaving Danville at seventeen to live with the party in Winston-Salem, recounts the party's 1966 founding by Huey Newton and Bobby Seale and its ten-point platform written in an anti-poverty office, and remembers serving in the free breakfast program, still feeling the heat of the griddles, in what he calls one of the happiest years of his life before Oakland.

chapters.title: Repression Breeds Resistance
chapters.summary: Hopkins frames the police brutality and Hoover's COINTELPRO campaign that had killed nearly thirty Panthers by 1971, the year he joined just after Huey Newton returned from prison, and the split when Eldridge Cleaver and the New York chapter pushed armed struggle and attacks on the police to heighten the contradiction with the oppressor.

chapters.title: Put Away the Gun
chapters.topic: Survival Programs and the Oakland Turn
chapters.summary: Hopkins recounts Newton's argument to put away the gun and keep it only for self-defense, his own lingering romance with the ideal of armed struggle, the survival programs of free groceries, busing to prisons, and medical clinics offered as survival till revolution, and the decision to work within the system as Bobby Seale ran for mayor of Oakland, a campaign that drew most chapters there and left them dismantled.

chapters.title: The Revolutionary War Correspondent
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes the party's dismantling and the migration to Oakland that carried him there in August 1972 to become the revolutionary war correspondent he had dreamed of being since reading of Clark Kent, joining within months the editorial cadre of five or six writers on the party newspaper alongside layout artists led by Emory Douglas.

chapters.title: The Son of Du Bois
chapters.summary: Hopkins remembers David Du Bois, stepson of W.E.B. Du Bois and son of Shirley Graham Du Bois, who had edited an English-language daily in Cairo for twelve years, became editor-in-chief of the paper and Hopkins's mentor and friend, taught him the fundamentals of journalism and the sensibilities of a literary artist, and wrote a blurb for his book before dying; Hopkins appeals to researchers to help locate Du Bois's papers on the party.

chapters.title: On the Bus With Bobby Seale
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes serving as writer and sound man on a reel-to-reel recorder, covering Bobby Seale's 1972-73 run for mayor of Oakland and Elaine Brown's city council bid, appearing barely visible in a newspaper photo holding a microphone near Seale and in a photographer's book at eighteen on a bus with Seale, an image that ran in Jet, and feeling he could reach the masses through a paper read by thousands.

chapters.title: Feet of Clay
chapters.summary: Hopkins reflects on forming his ideal of the heroic male and female through the party's strong women leaders, the disillusionment when the party fell apart and many heroes proved to have feet of clay, and his later reading on how the young mind keeps developing into the twenties, lending those years a bond reality in which they truly believed they could change the world.

chapters.title: You Can Never Leave
chapters.summary: Hopkins recalls a filmmaker shooting a documentary on the Seale campaign with the inventor of the Steadicam behind the camera, his first marijuana in California and the line from Hotel California about never being able to leave, and the positive illusion that let them believe they would take over Oakland until the campaign forced a runoff and they conceded the incumbent was too far ahead.

chapters.title: He Drew the Best Out of Me
chapters.topic: Du Bois and the Rhetoric of the Movement
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes David Du Bois as a learned man with a British accent who had lived in China and Cairo, inspired him toward social science, and edited his words to draw out a sense of style, color, and a cinematic eye for the humanity in detail, and reflects on the story, allegory, and poetic phrasing of the movement's preaching, observing that listening to Martin Luther King is almost like hearing a Shakespearean use of rhetoric.

chapters.title: Physical Methods of Correction
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes the party after the spring 1973 campaign as the Berlin Wall coming down, the loss of money for programs once the political donations stopped, Newton's inner core and security detail trying to hold power, and the physical methods of correction including the flogging of members that bred a totalitarian fear and began to unravel the fabric of the party.

chapters.title: Whose Ancestors Were Whipped
chapters.topic: A Stolen Car and a Mass Exodus
chapters.summary: Hopkins recounts the painful irony of an African American whose ancestors were whipped enduring the same inside the party, after he stole a car and fell in with an armed robber to raise money to bring his pregnant fiancee from Winston-Salem to California, then describes the central committee's authority being usurped, factions forming, and a mass exodus in which he happened to leave during the same weekend Huey Newton fled to Cuba after a 1977 pistol-whipping of his tailor and the shooting of a woman who later died.

chapters.title: Sisterhood Is Powerful
chapters.summary: Picking up after a break, Hopkins explains the party's philosophical ideal that women were equal to men and that gender inequality was part of the white patriarchal system, recalls that women may have made up nearly half the party and held many leadership positions though men kept most authority, and observes that women in the civil rights movement carried a greater role than women in the larger society.

chapters.title: Brought It Home to Their Kids
chapters.summary: Hopkins argues that black women were better educated and could withstand an educational system biased against black males, and that women who worked in white households gained cultural enrichment they brought home to develop their children and husbands, citing his own mother's college degree against his father's lack of secondary schooling as a source of equality of the sexes in the movement.

chapters.title: The Comedian and the Theoretician
chapters.summary: Hopkins gives his perspective on Bobby Seale, the highest leader he worked with directly, a stand-up comedian, jazz drummer, sheet-metal mechanic, and military veteran who brought organizational skills and drive to the party, contrasted with Huey Newton as the philosophical force and chief theoretician beloved for his brilliance and physical beauty.

chapters.title: The Life of an Outlaw
chapters.summary: Asked about the state's response to the Panthers and the expansion of incarceration, Hopkins recounts leaving Oakland so disillusioned and anti-everything that he wanted to live the life of an outlaw, robbed a bank and was sentenced to ten years, and after two years was returned to prison on a life sentence from an all-white Danville jury for a robbery in which no one was injured.

chapters.title: Standing Watch With a Shotgun
chapters.summary: Hopkins recalls joining the party in Winston-Salem while it was under attack by police and white supremacists, the previous headquarters firebombed, shootouts in Winston-Salem and an actual gun battle in High Point because members were told to defend their headquarters, and how all Panthers stood guard duty at night with a shotgun, an attitude he is unsure how to connect to his later turn toward criminality.

chapters.title: All Prisoners Are Political
chapters.topic: The Drug War and Mass Incarceration
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes a plank of the party platform holding that no black man got a fair trial so all should be freed as political prisoners, a position he does not fully share, and analyzes the rise of incarceration as driven by many causes, cultural, racial, and chiefly the drug war waged against people of color.

chapters.title: The Bastards of the Party
chapters.summary: Hopkins contends that in the absence of the Black Panther Party, as a filmmaker argued in a documentary called The Bastards of the Party, the attitude of resistance fractured into gang activity of Bloods against Crips, and that young people without a philosophy or an understanding of their history turn toward the hedonistic and tribal, glorifying guns with no understanding of what the guns were for.

chapters.title: The New Jim Crow
chapters.topic: A History People Do Not Know
chapters.summary: Hopkins frames incarceration as a degeneration the party never foresaw met by a larger community wanting to lock people up and throw away the key, cites a former New York official on white-collar criminals who steal more than common thieves, and laments that people do not know what the Black Panther Party, SNCC, Kwame Ture, or even the SDS were really about, mistaking them for hate-whitey movements.

chapters.title: An Idol or a Hero
chapters.summary: Hopkins distinguishes idols from heroes and laments a whitewashed history in which young people know no civil rights hero but Martin Luther King, recalls a Virginia native who helped found SNCC whose name he cannot remember, and argues for heritage enrichment and a sense of cultural justice beyond mere legal justice, teaching young people how to live rather than only training them for jobs.

chapters.title: Educate the Emotions
chapters.summary: Hopkins calls for understanding the harmony of interests among peoples and the contributions of Africans, Chinese, Germans, and Russians to culture, criticizes a politics that frames the future as a race against China rather than cooperation, and warns that stories now titillate rather than educate the emotions while children are taught to believe the metaphysical and supernatural they see on screens.

chapters.title: The Sons of Malcolm
chapters.summary: Hopkins explains that the party was founded just after Malcolm X's assassination and was nearly called the Sons of Malcolm, that Malcolm was developing a more ecumenical, worldly view of working within the system, and that the Black Panther Party should be seen as an extension of his philosophy, taking up the intercommunal idea that the world was more a group of communities than nations.

chapters.title: The Mind Is the Highest Form of Matter
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes the party's legacy as political but also one of pride and courage that debunked the notion of black inferiority, teaching that power is the ability to define a phenomenon and make it act in a desired fashion and that the mind is the highest form of organized matter, a continuation of Malcolm combined with Martin whose spirit of activism he sees in the election of Barack Obama and in Egypt.

chapters.title: The Power of Love
chapters.summary: Hopkins names the power of love and an ideal of service to something larger than oneself as part of the party's legacy against the self-hate in the black community, ties the idea of human progress to economic as well as cultural justice, and argues that the attacks on the union and service-worker movements show how far the country has drifted from the hard-fought gains of the labor movement.

chapters.title: Maggie L. Walker's School
chapters.summary: Hopkins argues that the Tea Party leads people to vote for tax cuts for the rich against their own interests in an almost idolatrous relationship rooted in race, then closes with the irony of a governor's school moved into the formerly segregated Maggie L. Walker High School, named for the turn-of-the-century black woman who founded a bank and a hundred-thousand-member benefit society, where a young white Republican spoke on television apparently unaware of Walker's history.

chapters.title: Poison Pen Letters
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes the FBI counterintelligence program launched against a so-called new black messiah, used against Martin Luther King and against the party through poison-pen letters that split Cleaver from Seale and helped imprison Elmer Geronimo Pratt, recounts learning that an agent told Winston-Salem State he was a Panther sent to infiltrate, costing him a scholarship and pushing him toward Oakland, and closes on the sense that they were never given a chance, George Jackson's line that revolution is against the law, and a comparison of the state's reaction to peaceful protest before the session ends.

loc_video.caption: Evans Derrell Hopkins oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Richmond, Virginia, 2011 July 07
